RONDO FORM | 7 EAST 7TH STREET| RIGHT MAKES MIGHT | INSPIRED BY "HEAVEN AND HELL" BY KANYE WEST

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1 — A: The Anchor]

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Cooper Union, seven East Seventh Street, brownstone standing tall" → Directly from: "Cooper Union, 7 East 7th Street at Cooper Square, tel, (212)-353-4100, www.cooper.edu." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 122.

MENTIONED AS: "The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art" / "Cooper Union Foundation Building" — "Italianate facade, wrought-iron beams inside the wall" → Drawn from: "A high-rise brownstone, Cooper Union is the oldest extant building framed with steel beams in America" and "The facade is in the Italianate brownstone tradition popular at the time." AIA. (1988) AIA Edition 3, p. 155.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Institute" — "Peter Cooper grew up poor, scanty means of culture known" → Directly from: "Its enlightened projector grew up in poverty, with scanty means of culture." Richmond. (1872) New York Institutions 1609-1872, p. 119.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Institute" — "Fruit of frugal toil, half a million dollars sewn" → Directly from: "the building is the fruit of frugal toil" and "cost nearly half a million." Richmond. (1872) New York Institutions 1609-1872, p. 118.

MENTIONED AS: "The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art" / "Cooper Union Foundation Building" — "Round elevator shaft — clairvoyant for his day" → Drawn from: "Anticipating the development of a passenger elevator, Cooper also installed a round elevator shaft in the building" and "a round elevator finally rides in his clairvoyantly round shaft." AIA. (1988) AIA Edition 3, p. 155.

MENTIONED AS: "The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art" / "Cooper Union Foundation Building" — "Oldest building framed with steel beams in America, they say" → Directly from: "Cooper Union is the oldest extant building framed with steel beams in America." AIA. (1988) AIA Edition 3, p. 155.

[Chorus — B: The Subject]

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Lincoln rose to speak, so tall, so angular, awkward on the stage" → Directly from: "When Lincoln rose to speak, I was greatly disappointed. He was tall, tall——oh, how tall! and so angular and awkward that I had, for an instant, a feeling of pity for so ungainly a man." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 122.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "His face lit up with inward fire, the whole man was transfigured in a rage" → Directly from: "his face lighted up as with an inward fire; the whole man was transfigured." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 122.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Right makes might, he told them all — dare to do our duty clear" → Directly from: "Let us have faith that right makes might, and in that faith let us, to the end, dare to do our duty as we understand it." Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 126.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" / "Cooper Institute" — "Brady and the Cooper Institute made him president from here" → Directly from: "'Brady and the Cooper Institute made me President'—Abraham Lincoln." Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 41.

[Verse 2 — A: Return to Anchor]

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Great Hall in the basement, fifteen hundred seats below" → Drawn from: "The hall held 1,500 people and was selected because it was one of the largest auditoriums in the city at the time." Morgan. (2013) The Civil War Lover's Guide to New York City, p. 50.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Institute" — "Most popular public hall in the city, echo after echo" → Directly from: "The large lecture-room in the basement is the most popular public hall in the city, and has echoed to the eloquence of the most noted men of this country." Richmond. (1872) New York Institutions 1609-1872, p. 118.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Graceful arches, columns, chandeliers from wall to wall" → Directly from: "Since it opened in 1859, with its graceful arches, columns, and chandeliers." Ackerman. (2016) Trotsky in New York 1917, p. 79.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Nine hundred seats where history answered every call" → Drawn from: "The Cooper Union hall held nine hundred seats." Ackerman. (2016) Trotsky in New York 1917, p. 79.

[Bridge — C: Secondary Context]

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Woodhull snuck past officers disguised, an old lady in a pearl-colored bonnet veiled" → Directly from: "she snuck past them disguised as 'an old lady in a pearl colored bonnet, with a veil and a black and white shawl.'" Holmes. (2004) 100 New Yorker, p. 222.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Trotsky spoke of Europe's graves while empty seats exhaled" → Drawn from: "The economic life of Europe is being blasted to its very foundations [by the world war], whereas America is increasing in wealth.... Will [Europe] not sink to nothing but a cemetery?" and "one witness remembered seeing plenty of empty seats." Ackerman. (2016) Trotsky in New York 1917, p. 79.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Damrosch called the working people, two thousand jammed the door" → Drawn from: "A handbill addressed to 'The Working People of New York' was distributed" and "more than two thousand men and women of the Lower East Side—Germans, Russian Jews, Irish, Italians, Poles, and others—had come to the People's Singing Classes." Bender. (1987) New York Intellect, p. 217.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Ten cents a lesson, classical not popular — that's what freedom's for" → Drawn from: "the assembled crowd voted, after he told them he could get the necessary funds from philanthropists, for independence and a fee of ten cents per lesson. They also voted, at a subsequent meeting, to learn classical rather than popular music." Bender. (1987) New York Intellect, p. 217.

[Outro — A: Final Synthesis]

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union Institute" — "Douglass made the rafters ring, abolitionists held the floor" → Directly from: "Frederick Douglass made the rafters ring on many occasions, and Wendell Phillips and other abolitionists also spoke here." Harris. (1968) A Negro History Tour of Manhattan, p. 58.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Red Cross, NAACP were founded right behind that door" → Directly from: "The American Red Cross, Volunteers of America, and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) were all founded in this hall." Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 126.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union Library" / "Cooper Union" — "From the busy fires of Jim's boiler room to the Great Hall's sacred light" → Drawn from: "as I watched the busy flames under Jim's boilers" and "I related to him the story of James Watt and his experiments with the steam-engine, a story which I had dug out in an old encyclopaedia in the Cooper Union Library." Pupin. (1923) From Immigrant to Inventor, p. 56.

MENTIONED AS: "Cooper Union" — "Cooper Union, seven East Seventh — where right still makes might" → Drawn from: "Let us have faith that right makes might." Bergman. (2001) The Spiritual Traveler New York City, p. 126.
